coredata.dc:title: Efficient synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots for cell imaging using unripe fruit extract of Prunus mume
coredata.dc:creator: Atchudan, Raji
Edison, Thomas Nesakumar Jebakumar Immanuel
Sethuraman, Mathur Gopalakrishnan
Lee, Yong Rok
coredata.dc:description: Highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were synthesized using the extract of unripe Prunus mume (P. mume) fruit by a simple one step hydrothermal-carbonization method. The N-CDs were synthesized at different pH ranges, 2.3, 5, 7, and 9. The pH of the P. mume extract was adjusted using an aqueous ammonia solution (25%). The optical properties of N-CDs were examined by UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. The N-CDs synthesized at pH 9 emitted high fluorescence intensity compared to other obtained N-CDs. The N-CDs synthesized at pH 9 was further characterized by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Raman spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and Fourier transform-infra red (FT-IR) spectroscopy. HR-TEM showed that the average size of the synthesized N-CDs was approximately 9nm and the interlayer distance was 0.21nm, which was validated by XRD. The graphitic nature of the synthesized N-CDs were confirmed by Raman spectroscopy. XPS and FT-IR spectroscopy confirmed the doping of the nitrogen moiety over the synthesized CDs. The synthesized nitrogen doped CDs (N-CDs) were low toxicity and were used as a staining probe for fluorescence cell imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Prunus mume
Nitrogen-doped carbon dot
Hydrothermal
Carbonization
Optical property
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of P. mume fruit extract

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Korean Ministry of Education , Science, and Technology
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Education, Science and Technology
Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the Nano Material Technology Development Program of the Korean National Research Foundation (NRF) funded by the Korean Ministry of Education , Science, and Technology ( 2012M3A7B4049675 ). This work was also supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea government (MSIP) ( NRF-2014R1A2A1A11052391 ) and Priority Research Centers Program ( 2014R1A6A1031189 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram for the preparation of N-CDs.
(a) UV–vis and (b) fluorescence spectra of P. mume fruit extract in water with different pH.
(a) UV–vis, and (b) fluorescence spectra of N-CDs-1, N-CDs-2, N-CDs-3 and N-CDs-4 in water.
Excitation-dependent fluorescence spectra of (a) N-CDs-4, (b) N-CDs-3 (c) N-CDs-2 and (d) N-CDs-1.
Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs-4 excited with different solvents.
TEM images of (a) N-CDs-2, (b) N-CDs-3, (c) and (d) N-CDs-4 (Inset of (a), (b) and (c) is size distribution of corresponding N-CDs).
(a) Wide-angle XRD pattern, and (b) Raman spectra of N-CDs-4.
FT-IR spectra of the P. mume fruit extract and the N-CDs-4.
(a) XPS full scan survey spectrum of N-CDs-4, High resolution XPS spectrum of (b) C1s, (c) N1s, and (d) O1s for the synthesized N-CDs-4.
CKK-8 cytotoxicity assay of MDA-MB-231 cells incubated with N-CDs-4 at different concentrations for 24h.

Fluorescent microscopy images of MDA-MB-231 cells after the cellular uptake of CDs (20μg/mL) for 4h. The ultra violet excitation and the emission were obtained by fluorescent microscopy (a) without N-CDs-4 in bright field, (b) without N-CDs-4 in fluorescence, (c) with N-CDs-4 in bright field using DMSO, (d) with N-CDs-4 in fluorescence using DMSO, (e) with N-CDs-4 in bright field using water and (f) with N-CDs-4 in fluorescence using water.
Comparison of optical characteristics and quantum yield of the CDs derived from different carbon precursor with the reported methods.
The percentage of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen for the synthesized N-CDs with different pH, the results obtained from XPS analysis.

head.ce:title: Efficient synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots for cell imaging using unripe fruit extract of Prunus mume

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, Yeungnam University, Gyeongsan 38541, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Gandhigram Rural Institute-Deemed University, Gandhigram 624 302, Tamilnadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The green synthesis of highly fluorescent N-CDs was achieved using the extract of unripe P. mume fruit as a carbon precursor by a one-pot simple hydrothermal-carbonization method. The resulting N-CDs were used as a staining agent for the fluorescence imaging of MDA-MB-231 cells.

Highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were synthesized using the extract of unripe Prunus mume (P. mume) fruit by a simple one step hydrothermal-carbonization method. The N-CDs were synthesized at different pH ranges, 2.3, 5, 7, and 9. The pH of the P. mume extract was adjusted using an aqueous ammonia solution (25%). The optical properties of N-CDs were examined by UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. The N-CDs synthesized at pH 9 emitted high fluorescence intensity compared to other obtained N-CDs. The N-CDs synthesized at pH 9 was further characterized by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Raman spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and Fourier transform-infra red (FT-IR) spectroscopy. HR-TEM showed that the average size of the synthesized N-CDs was approximately 9nm and the interlayer distance was 0.21nm, which was validated by XRD. The graphitic nature of the synthesized N-CDs were confirmed by Raman spectroscopy. XPS and FT-IR spectroscopy confirmed the doping of the nitrogen moiety over the synthesized CDs. The synthesized nitrogen doped CDs (N-CDs) were low toxicity and were used as a staining probe for fluorescence cell imaging.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) with sizes below 10nm are a new class of carbon nanomaterials [1]. These CDs have attracted considerable attention because of their excellent properties, such as fluorescence properties, which make its applicability in fluorescence cell imaging, energy, catalysis, optics, and sensors [2–9]. CDs have low cytotoxicity, are eco-friendly, and have good biocompatibility, aqueous solubility and high photostability compared to fluorescent dyes and semiconductor quantum dots. Therefore, CDs are a good alternative for fluorescent dyes and semiconductor quantum dots for cell imaging and biosensing applications [10,11]. The fluorescence properties of CDs depend on their size, pH, passivating agent, defects, and solvent [12,13]. Currently, the synthesis of CDs with high fluorescence intensity and different emission properties are a challenging task for nanomaterial researchers. In general, CDs are synthesized using either top-down or bottom-up approaches. The top-down approaches containing arc-discharge, laser ablation and electrochemical methods have been used for the synthesis of CDs. Bottom-up approaches include microwave-assisted carbonization, hydrothermal-carbonization and aqueous based methods. In the top-down methods, graphite or multi-walled carbon nanotubes are used as carbon precursors, whereas in the bottom-up method, carbohydrates or simple organic acids are used as the carbon precursor [14–17]. Among them, bottom-up methods are quite simple, facile, cost effective, and eco-friendly. In particular, the hydrothermal-carbonization method is simple and large scale with a narrow size distribution for the preparation of CDs. The biomass of fruits, vegetables, milk, hair, caramel, and honey has been utilized as green carbon precursors for the synthesis of fluorescent CDs and N-CDs [18–26].

In general, the plant extracts containing various bioactive molecules such as acidic constituents (tannin, polyphenols, flavonoids, citric acid, tartaric acid, and ascorbic acid), base constituents (alkaloids), and neutral constituents (carbohydrates) are intriguing when applied in a nanoparticle synthesis. When applied for the nanoparticle synthesis (like carbon dots), the synthetic kinetics sometimes depends on the phytoconstituents present in the particular extract. The extract phytoconstituents present does not only determine the surface functional groups on the carbon dots but also the reactivity of the material. Biswal et al., have investigated the supercapacitor behaviour of carbon particles derived from neem leaf and ashoka leaf. They showed that particles derived from neem leaf have higher supercapacity than those from the ashoka due to the different phytoconstituents present in the neem leaf [27]. Likewise, in CDs, the fluorescence quantum yield (fluorescence intensity) also depends on the type of phytoconstituents, size of particles, solvents, and dopants. This behaviour opens the way for the application of CDs as fluorescent probes for the selective and sensitive detection of metal cations [28], thus making the use of plant extracts an amusing aspect in the CD synthesis. Exploiting the idea, CD synthesis from the P. mume fruit is reported and discussed in this work. P. mume is a deciduous tree belonging to the Rosaceae family and is cultivated widely in China, Japan and South Korea. P. mume fruits are easily available and affordable, and were consumed as food and medicinal materials. The major phytoconstituents present in the fruits are epicatechin, isoquercitrin, rutin, quercetin 3-O-neohesperidoside, and (−)-epicatechin. The immature unripe fruits of P. mume have been used in antitussive, expectoration, antiemetic, antidiarrheal, anthelmintic, and antipyretic treatments [29–31]. The sucrose and glucose were dominant sugars, and citric acid was dominant organic acids in P. mume fruits, this might be helpful to formation of carbon materials. In addition, the fermented P. mume fruits can meet the standard to be further processed into prune or sauces, and the fermentation broth of P. mume fruits with Lactobacillus fermentium have a good prospect in the development of probiotic beverage [32]. The composition of P. mume is quite different from that of extracts of other fruits including orange and lemon, which was reported previously for the synthesis of CDs and N-CDs. Hence, the P. mume fruits will be chosen as an ideal source for the preparation of N-CDs in this work.

This paper reports the green synthesis of highly fluorescent N-CDs using the unripe fruit extract of P. mume as a carbon precursor via a simple hydrothermal-carbonization method. The N-CDs were synthesized at different pH, such as 2.3 (fruit extract as it is), 5, 7, and 9 (pH was adjusted by aqueous ammonia). The optical properties of the obtained N-CDs were studied by UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. The highly fluorescent N-CDs were characterized by high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Raman spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and Fourier transform-infra red (FT-IR) spectroscopy. The highly fluorescent N-CDs were used as a staining agent for the fluorescence imaging of MDA-MB-231 cells. To the best of our knowledge, P. mume is first time used for the synthesis of N-CDs and as a staining probe for fluorescence cell imaging.

The unripe P. mume fruits were procured from the Yeungnam University campus, Gyeongsan, Republic of Korea. An aqueous ammonia solution (25%), acetonitrile, ethanol and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and the aqueous ammonia solution was used to adjust the pH of the extract of P. mume. The MDA-MB-231 cells were purchased from the Korean Cell Line Bank. The entire chemicals were used as received. The deionized (DI) water was used in this work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of P. mume fruit extract
ce:section.ce:para: The unripe fruits of P. mume were washed thoroughly with DI water and cut into small slices without seed. An appropriate amount (150g) of sliced unripe fruit was ground well using a mixer grinder. The grounded P. mume was filtered through cotton followed by Whatman filter paper. The fruit extract was collected and the pH of the extract was measured using a WTW Inolab pH 720pH meter. The pH of the obtained extract was approximately 2.3 and the obtained extract was used for the synthesis of N-CDs.

The pH of the fruit extract was adjusted by adding an aqueous ammonia solution. The pH-adjusted fruit extracts (pH: 5, 7 and 9) and plain fruit extract (pH: 2.3) were used to synthesize the N-CDs by a hydrothermal-carbonization method. In a typical synthesis, 30mL of P. mume fruit extract was taken in to a Teflon cup with a 50mL inner volume and slid into a stainless steel (SS) autoclave (The photographic images of autoclave with Teflon cup which is used for the hydrothermal process is shown in Fig. S1). The autoclave was sealed carefully and heated to 180°C for 5h in a hot air oven. After hydrothermal-carbonization, the reaction mixture was allowed to cool to room temperature. A similar synthesis procedure was adapted for the other pH of the fruit extracts (The photographic images of the as-synthesized N-CDs at different pH are shown in Fig. S2). The obtained dark brown (blackish) solutions were centrifuged at 10,000rpm for 20min. The N-CD-containing supernatant solutions were collected and went under centrifugation. Afterwards, the brown coloured supernatant solution was dialyzed in DI water for 24h using cellulose acetate membrane with molecular weight cut off 1000Da, wherein every 30min the water was changed. The resulted N-CD-containing solutions were collected and dried using a freeze-dryer. The dried solid materials were stored for further use. The CDs synthesized at different pH, such as 2.3, 5, 7, and 9 are denoted as N-CDs-1, N-CDs-2, N-CDs-3 and N-CDs-4, respectively. Fig. 1 shows a schematic illustration of the synthesis procedure for the N-CDs.

To find the best candidates for cell imaging, the highly fluorescent N-CDs were identified. The optical properties of the N-CDs were analyzed using an OPTIZEN 3220UV and Hitachi-7000F spectrometer. The excitation wavelength was varied from lower to higher wavelength to find the maximum luminescence intensity for the fluorescence measurements. The slit with was fixed as 5nm and the scan rate was set to 240nm/min. The N-CDs-4 showed high fluorescence emission intensity and were further characterized and assessed for their applications.

One drop of a N-CDs (N-CDs-1, N-CDs-2, N-CDs-3 and N-CDs-4) suspension was casted onto a carbon coated polymer micro grid supported on a Cu grid and dried at ambient temperature. The HR-TEM (FEI-Tecnai TF-20) images were taken at an acceleration voltage of 200kV.
For XRD analysis, the diffraction pattern was obtained using a PANalytical X'Pert3 MRD diffractometer (CuKα radiation (λ=1.54Å) at 40kV and 30mA).
Raman spectrum was recorded on a thermo scientific DXR SmartRaman spectrometer with laser excitation at 532nm.

The surface functionalization of the synthesized N-CDs was investigated using K-Alpha (Thermo Scientific) X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) and ATR-FTIR spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer Spectrum Two).

CCK-8 cell viability assay: For the cell viability study, the MDA-MB231 cells cultured in the presence of CDs were determined using the CCK-8 assay. Each well was plated with 5×103 MDA-MB231 cells in 96 well plates. Subsequently, overnight incubation of various concentrations of N-CDs (0, 5, 10, 15, 20, and 25μg/mL) at 37°C in a 5.0% CO2/95% air incubator. All the samples had four parallel wells. After culturing for 24h, 10μL of CCK-8 solution was added and incubated for 4h and then read at 450nm using a microplate reader (Bio-rad).

Fluorescence imaging: The cellular uptake of the CDs was determined by fluorescence microscopy. The MDA-MB-231 cells were cultured in Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM)-High containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1% antibiotic-antimycotic (Gibco) at 37°C incubator with 5.0% CO2 and 95% air. After 4h incubation with 20μg/mL CDs, the medium was removed and the cells were washed three times with phosphate buffered saline and then analyzed for fluorescent microscopy (Nikon eclipse Ti) at ultraviolet excitation and emission.

UV–vis spectroscopy is a useful tool for studying the optical properties of CDs and it is complementary to fluorescence spectroscopy [33]. Fig. 2 shows the UV–vis and fluorescence spectra of the carbon precursor (extract of unripe P. mume fruit) in water with different pH. The UV–vis spectra showed that an extract of unripe P. mume fruit absorbed at 285 and 317nm at pH of 2.3, 5, and 7. The absorbance intensities of P. mume fruit extract at pH 5 and 7 were lower than that of pH 2.3. On the other hand (P. mume fruit extract with pH 9), the absorbance spectrum was changed significantly, which is due to the conversion of all the phenolic OH to corresponding ammonium phenolate ions. The changes in absorbance of P. mume fruit extract at various pH are reflected in the fluorescence spectra. Fig. 2(b) show the fluorescence spectra of the P. mume fruit extract at various pH. The fluorescence intensity of the P. mume fruit extract was very low at pH 2.3. The emission intensity of the P. mume fruit extract increased gradually from pH 5–9. Among them, pH 9 resulted in a higher emission intensity. This confirms that the pH of the P. mume fruit extract influences the fluorescence intensity. In addition, the extract of P. mume fruit at pH 2.3, 5, 7, and 9, were carbonized via hydrothermal method.

The N-CDs were synthesized at different pH, such as 2.25 (fruit extract as it is), 5, 7, and 9 of the P. mume fruit extract. Fig. 3 presents the UV–vis and its corresponding fluorescence spectra of the synthesized N-CDs in water at different pH. The UV–vis spectra (Fig. 3(a)) show that the N-CDs-1 absorbed at 282nm but the other N-CDs-2, N-CDs-3 and N-CDs-4 absorbed at 331nm, which is due to the n-π* transitions of the carbonyl group or amine moiety present in the P. mume extract on the CDs [34]. In addition, the absorbance intensity of N-CDs-4 was higher than that of the other N-CDs, which indicates the increasing of concentration of N-CDs at pH 9. Fig. 3(b) presents the corresponding fluorescence spectra (excitation at 331nm) of all N-CDs. The synthesized N-CDs-1, N-CDs-2, N-CDs-3 and N-CDs-4 emitted fluorescence at 422, 424, 435 and 435nm, respectively. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs increased with increasing pH (from acidic to basic pH). This suggests that the N-CDs-4 has higher fluorescence intensity than other N-CDs, which might be due to the high doping of nitrogen from an aqueous ammonia solution to CDs and also the very small sized N-CDs synthesized at pH 9 (The smaller size of N-CDs-4 was confirmed by TEM images of Fig. 6(c)). The excitation dependent fluorescence emission properties of all the synthesized N-CDs were also studied. Fig. 4 shows the fluorescence spectra of N-CDs-1, N-CDs-2, N-CDs-3 and N-CDs-4. The fluorescence intensity of the synthesized N-CDs at different pH were noted and are presented in the ascending order of N-CDs–1<N-CDs–2<N-CDs–3<N-CDs-4. The synthesized N-CDs-4, shows a high fluorescence intensity with excitation at 355nm. As a control experiment (without nitrogen dopant-NH4OH), the N-CDs-Na was synthesized using NaOH as pH modifier (see supporting information for the detailed synthesize procedure). Fluorescence intensity was low for N-CDs-Na compared to fluorescence intensity observed for N-CDs-4 (see Fig. S3). This is might due to the absence of nitrogen dopant in N-CDs-Na. Therefore, N-CDs-4 was taken as the best candidate for further characterization and applications. We obtained the quantum yield of the synthesized N-CDs-4 (unknown molecular weight) by the following Eq. (1) [35,36]:
(1)
QY CDs
=
QY R
I CDs
A R
( n
CDs
)
2
I R
A CDs
( n
R
)
2
where, QYCDs and QYR are the fluorescence quantum yield of CDs and reference, respectively. (Reference: Quinine sulphate, QYR =0.54), ICDs and IR is the integrated fluorescence intensity of CDs and reference, respectively. ACDs and AR is the UV–vis absorption intensity of CDs (0.747) and reference (0.745), respectively. n is the refractive index (1) of solvent for both CDs and reference. The calculated quantum yield of CDs-9 was around 16%. Table 1 shows the comparison of the quantum yield of the synthesized N-CDs derived from various carbon sources such as fruit extract and chemical compounds. The CDs synthesized using extract of unripe P. mume fruit produced high florescence quantum yield then compared to the reported quantum yield [37–42].

The effect of solvents, such as acetonitrile, DMSO, ethanol, and DI water on the fluorescence of N-CDs-4 was studied, and the corresponding fluorescence spectra were shown in Fig. 5 . Water and DMSO emits strong fluorescence at 446 and 478nm with the same intensity compared to other solvents, which are clear from the fluorescence spectra. Fig. 5 presents the visual observation of N-CDs-4 in water and DMSO under the table-top UV-Lamp (365nm) (inset photographic images). In addition, N-CDs (N-CDs-1, N-CDs-2, N-CDs-3 and N-CDs-4) were characterized by HR-TEM, XRD, XPS, FT-IR spectroscopy, and Raman spectroscopy.

Fig. 6 presents TEM images of N-CDs-2, N-CDs-3 and N-CDs-4. The spherical N-CDs-4 with a narrow size distribution can be seen clearly. The size distributions of the N-CDs-2, CDs-3 and CDs-4 were calculated from the TEM images Fig. 6(a), (b) and (c), respectively, as shown in inset of corresponding images. The size distribution histogram shows that the N-CDs-4 had a narrow size distribution compared to N-CDs-2 and N-CDs-3. The resulting N-CDs-4 diameter ranges from 7 to 11nm with an average size of 9nm. The lattice fringes of the N-CDs-4 were clearly seen in the high resolution TEM image (Fig. 6(d)). The ordered lattice fringes confirmed that the synthesized N-CDs were graphitic. The calculated interlayer distance (d-spacing value) of the N-CDs was approximately 0.21nm, shows good agreement with XRD results.

The phase structure and degree of crystallinity of the synthesized N-CDs was determined by XRD. Fig. 7 (a) shows a typical wide-angle XRD pattern of the N-CDs-4. The pattern clearly showed an intense diffraction peak approximately 2θ at 23.5°, which can be indexed to the C(002) plane and the weak XRD peaks at approximately 43 and 78° 2θ were assigned to the C(101) and C(110) planes, respectively [42–46]. All the XRD peaks revealed the graphitic nature of N-CDs. The interlayer distance (d-spacing value) was calculated using the Bragg Eq. (2) based on the position of the C(002) and C(101) reflection peak [47,48]. The calculated interlayer distance was approximately 0.37 and 0.21nm for the reflection peak of C(002) and C(101), respectively. The calculated interlayer distance matched well with the result obtained from HR-TEM.
(2)
n = 2dsin θ
( or )
d =
n λ
2sin θ
where, n is a positive integer (1), λ is the wavelength of incident X-ray (λ=1.54Å) and θ is the angle between the incident rays and the surface of the materials.

Raman spectroscopy is one of the most powerful non-invasive tools for the characterization of carbon nanostructured materials. Fig. 7 (b) shows the Raman spectra of the N-CDs-4. Graphitic carbon normally has two common bands below 2000cm−1 in its Raman spectrum, i.e., D-band and G-band. The D-band is related to defects in the graphitic sheets and the G-band is related to the vibration of the sp2-hybridized carbon atoms in the graphitic sheets. The peak for the disorder-induced D-band was approximately at 1350cm−1 and those for the G-bands were observed around 1570cm−1 [49,50]. The peak intensities of the D-band and G-band are denoted as ID and IG , respectively. The graphitization of carbon nanostructured materials was determined by ID/IG . The ID/IG values were around 0.75 and the low intensity ratio of the D to G band (ID
<
IG ) indicates that the N-CDs obtained are graphitized [51]. The data shows that the N-CDs were synthesized without major carbonaceous impurities [52–54].

Fig. 8 presents the surface functionality of the synthesized N-CDs was examined by ATR-FT-IR spectroscopy. The FT-IR spectra of synthesized N-CDs-4 and its precursor (extract of P. mume fruit). The FT-IR spectrum of the P. mume fruit extract shows the major absorption bands at 3418, 2949, 1713, and 1177cm−1, which correspond to the stretching frequency of phenolic OH/NH, −CH, CO, and COC functional groups, respectively [55] present in P. mume. The OH stretching band was shifted from 3418 to 3267cm−1 for the synthesized N-CDs, which may be due to the conversion of phenolic OH to ammonium phenolate (So, more dopant (nitrogen moieties) will be over the N-CDs, NH bond formation). The stretching vibration of the −CH functional group shifted from 2949 to 2924cm−1. The broad absorption band appeared at 1610cm−1 for the synthesized N-CDs-4, which is characteristic vibration of the sp2 CC stretching mode. The FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs-4 showed absorption bands at 3267, 2924, 1610, and 1267cm−1, which suggests that all the functional groups present in the extract of P. mume had shifted position and addition of nitrogen group present in the N-CDs. Moreover, the intensity of the bands of CO and COC decreased, shifted and broadened, which may be due to the influence of carbonyl groups in the synthesis of N-CDs. This suggests that the synthesized N-CDs were functionalized by phenolic OH, ammonium phenolate (NH,) and carbonyl groups.

The functionality of the N-CDs was confirmed by XPS. Fig. 9 shows the XPS survey scan spectrum of N-CDs-4. Three binding energy was observed at 534.08, 401.08 and 286.08eV, which were assigned to the 1s orbitals of oxygen, nitrogen and carbon atoms, respectively. Fig. 9(b)–(d) show the high resolution XPS spectra of O (1s), N (1s) and C (1s), respectively. The XPS spectra of C (1s) consisted of three binding energy appeared at 284.48, 285.68 and 286.88eV, which correspond to the CC (sp2), CN and CO/CO functional groups, respectively [56–58]. The XPS spectrum of N (1s) yielded a single binding peak at 400.58eV, showing the presence of CN group on the surface of CDs [59]. In addition, XPS of O (1s) showed a binding peak at 532.88eV, which confirmed the presence of CO functional groups in the synthesized N-CDs [60]. Moreover, XPS strongly suggested that the synthesized N-CDs-4 were functionalized by CO/CO and CN groups, which are present in the extract of the P. mume fruit at pH 9. Fig. S4 shows the XPS spectra of N-CDs at different pH (N-CDs-1, N-CDs-2, N-CDs-3 and N-CDs-4). The percentage of nitrogen increased over the CDs from N-CDs-1 to N-CDs-4, this is due to the increase in amount of ammonia solution. The atomic percentage of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen for the synthesized N-CDs-1, N-CDs-2, N-CDs-3 and N-CDs-4 are listed in Table 2 . The atomic percentage of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen is approximately 80.11, 5.97 and 13.92%, respectively for N-CDs-4. Among the synthesized N-CDs, the N-CDs-4 exhibits more nitrogen evident from XPS analysis. Fig. S5 shows the XPS spectra of N-CDs-Na at pH 9. The nitrogen content is low when compared to N-CDs-4, which is due to the absence of nitrogen dopant in N-CDs-Na. However, trace amount of nitrogen is observed for N-CDs-Na is from the fruit extract.

All the physico-chemical characterization techniques, confirmed the structure and properties of N-CDs. The highly fluorescent N-CDs-4 was selected as a better candidate and was used as a probe for cell imaging in fluorescence microscopy. The biocompatibility of the synthesized N-CDs-4 was investigated using a CKK-8 assay to assess the cytotoxicity of N-CDs on MDA-MB-231 cells. The results are shown in Fig. 10 . The cell viability was barely affected when the N-CDs concentration was varied from 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, and 25μg/mL for 24h. The cell survival rates still exceeded 96% at all experimental concentrations, indicating that the N-CDs have no apparent toxicity and are cyto-compatible with the MDA-MB-231 cells, which makes them potential candidates for imaging in biosensors and in vivo studies [40]. Based on the excellent photoluminescence properties and low cytotoxicity, N-CDs can serve as promising probes for cell imaging applications. Here the MDA-MB-231 cells were incubated with the N-CDs-4 at a concentration of 20μg/mL and the imaging performance was examined to assess the cell viability by fluorescence microscopy. Fig. 11 shows the fluorescence microscopy images of the MDA-MB-231 cells in the absence and presence of N-CDs-4 with water and DMSO medium. The N-CDs-4 containing MDA-MB-231 cells were clearly seen when compared to the control, which suggests that the synthesized N-CDs have good compatibility and permeability to the cell wall of MDA-MB-231. The fluorescence microscopic image also reveals that the synthesized N-CDs-4 had high photostability with no blinking [61–65]. The fluorescence microscopy images of the N-CDs in water and DMSO showed a blue color reflecting green color reflecting, respectively. The synthesized N-CDs-4 were internalized into MDA-MB-231 cells and showed bright blue and green fluorescence at UV light excitation, which could be useful as cell imaging agents.

This paper reported a simple, cost effective and eco-friendly route for the synthesis of highly fluorescent N-CDs using the extract of P. mume unripe fruit in water by a simple one pot hydrothermal-carbonization method. The N-CDs were synthesized using P. mume fruit extract at various pH at 180°C. All the synthesized N-CDs showed fluorescence nature. In addition, the fluorescence intensity increased with increasing pH of the P. mume extract from acidic to basic. The intensity difference was attributed to the amount of nitrogen doping at different pH ranges. The CDs synthesized at pH 9 emitted a maximum fluorescence intensity at 440nm, which showed that the level of nitrogen doping is high at pH 9. The nitrogen doping on the N-CDs was confirmed by FT-IR and XPS. The degree of graphitization was confirmed by XRD and Raman spectroscopy. The interlayer distance of the N-CDs was approximately 0.21nm calculated from the HR-TEM image, which is consistent with the XRD results. The highly fluorescent N-CDs-4 were used as a staining probe for the fluorescence imaging of MDA-MB-231 cells. Moreover, the highly fluorescent N-CDs-4 were synthesized with quantum yield of 16% and low toxicity from a green chemical route and used as excellent imaging probes for biomedical applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Nano Material Technology Development Program of the Korean National Research Foundation (NRF) funded by the Korean Ministry of Education, Science, and Technology (2012M3A7B4049675). This work was also supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea government (MSIP) (NRF-2014R1A2A1A11052391) and Priority Research Centers Program (2014R1A6A1031189).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2016.05.054.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:
